titleStmt.title: Revisions to Henry David Thoreau's manuscript of Walden

publicationStmt.p: Transcriptions from HM 924, the manuscript of Walden:

sourceDesc.bibl: The first sentence of what would become paragraph 5 of the "Conclusion" to Walden makes its initial appearance in Draft E. Thoreau makes additional changes to the sentence in F. In the excerpts below from the manuscript pages where the sentence appears, we see Thoreau inserting and deleting various adjectives, adverbs, and phrases that qualify his assertions about what he learned from his experience at the pond. Some of his changes also seem intended to tighten up the wording and increase the impact of what he has to say. In E, many of Thoreau's revisions are in pencil. In F they are almost all in ink.

zone.seg:
To the elevation and ennoblement of mankind
what are called the luxuries and many of the comforts
of life are not only not indispensable, but positive
hindrances. With respect to luxuries and comforts,
the wisest have ever lived a more simple and
meager life than the poor. The ancient
philosophers, were a class of men than whom none
were"have been" interlined in ink poorer in respect to outward
riches, none so rich in inward. We know not much
about them. It is astonishing that "we" underlined can know so
much as we do The same is true of the more
modern reformers and benefactors of their race.
None can be an impartial or wise observer of
human life but from the vantage ground of what "we" italicized
should call voluntary poverty. Of a life of luxury the
fruit is luxury, whether in agriculture, or commerce,
or literature, or art. At the top of this page from E, Thoreau has made many changes in pencil. What did he learn? "I learned this by my experiment" becomes "I learned this at least by my experiment." Implication: he learned many things but wants to single out this one thing in conclusion. The qualifying words "endeavors to," "quite," "wholly," and "in a measure" seem to make his claim about finding success by advancing "confidently" a little more–to pick up on his own word—measured.

To the elevation and ennoblement of mankind
what are called, interlined in pencil "most of" the luxuries and many of
the interlined in pencil "so-called" comforts of life are not only not
indispensable, but positive hinderances to the "e" in hinderances and "to the" interlined in pencil
"elevation of mankind." interlined in pencil With respect to luxuries and
comforts, the wisest have ever lived a more simple
and meager life than the poor. The ancient
philosophers, "Chinese, Hindoo, Persian, and Greek," interlined in pencil and I may add Christ as a more poplar
example perhaps generally were a class of men
than which none have been poorer in respect to
outward riches, none so rich in inward. We know
not much about them. It is astonishing "remarkable" interlined in pencil
that "we" underlined can know so much "of them" interlined in pencil as we do.
"The same is true of the most important reformers" interlined in pencil that
have livedThe same is true of the more modern
reformers and benefactors of their race. None can
be an impartial or wise observer of human life but
from the vantage ground of what "we" italicized should call
voluntary poverty. Of a life of luxury the fruit is
luxury, whether in agriculture, or commerce, or
literature, or art.
Critics have been very lavish
of the word philosopher of late. According to them
every century has had several. But we have
forgotten what the name implies. These men were
perhaps"We hear nowadays of" interlined in pencil professors of
philisophy, "of" interlined in pencil readers of it, sometimes even "of" interlined in pencil
utterers of it to a slight extent, but never "of" interlined in pencil livers of
it. But it is admirable to profess, to"or" interlined in pencil read, to"or" interlined in pencil
utter, simply because it was once admirable to live.
To be a philosopher is not even to have subtle
thoughts, and found a school, merely, but, what is
"infinitely" interlined in pencilinfinitely rarer still, to live a life of simplicity, "of" interlined in ink
independence, "of" interlined in ink magnanimity and trust, such as
the weak, the unwise and the dependent can not
live. With the actual life of man for the problem,
to see how you can solve it!"few have ever lived. It is" interlined in pencil
"to solve some of the problems of life both" interlined in pencil
"theoretically and practically" interlined in pencil Some modern men "who" interlined in pencil
"have copied the title of philosopher" interlined in pencil have "had" interlined in inkskill
and ambition "and skill" interlined in pencil enough to lead partially
successful and pleasing lives under the
circumstances, but there is no bending of
circumstances under their hands. It is"considering" interlined in pencil
"their circumstances, but theirs was" interlines in pencil at best a
courtier like success, not kingly, not manly. We are
pigmies and dwarfs."They have made shift to live" interlined in pencil
"merely by conformity and a kind of flattery of fate" interlined in pencil
Where are the progenitors of a nobler race of men?
-the founders of nations? Why do"It would be well" interlined in pencil
"to ask ourselves why" interlined in pencil men degenerate ever? What
makes families run out? What is the nature of that
luxury that"which" interlined in pencil enervates and destroys nations?
And is there"are we sure that there is" interlined in pencil none of it in
our "own" interlined in pencil lives? As I have said the philosopher is in
advance of his age not merely in his discourse, but
in his life, even in the "outward" interlined in ink form and outward
mode if it. He is not fed, sheltered, clothed,
warmed, like his fellows. How can a man be a
philosopher and not maintain his vital heat by
better methods than other men?
After re-copying the sentence in F, Thoreau continues to revise, this time almost entirely in ink. (The word "and" is deleted in pencil as well as ink, and what appears to have been a pencilled insertion between "advances" and "confidently" has been rubbed out.) Noe that the final words of the sentence have been deleted, with their reference to a "result" answering to "faith," the sentence puts more emphasis on "success." (The emphasis is enhanced, too, by Thoreau's decision to strike "quite unexpected.") However, Thoreau retains "endeavors to," which he first inserted in E. While the sentence gets a little bolder in F, then, it retains the idea that endeavoring to life your imagined life is the important thing. Striving itself might be regarded as a kind of success.
